story:
Cindy tugs her Grammy through a department store, impatient for a party dress. Mabel, steady behind the stroller with baby Josh, detours into cosmetics. Can never find the right lipstick. Stay two steps ahead, she thinks. They may be quick, but I’m smarter and stronger. Cindy spies around a display. “Don’t run off,” Mabel warns. “Talk to your brother.” “He’s a baby,” Cindy snaps. “Are you a baby? Or a brat?” A hand to the mouth; a mumbled “sorry.” Mabel reaches for a tester, tries a color on her hand. This is it. I should get two.

Crash. Cindy’s pushed the stroller into a display, sending compacts and tubes skittering. Josh winds up to a full cry. Heads turn. Mabel scoops Josh, swallows a yell. “Cindy, what are you doing? This isn’t a playground.” Tears threaten; Cindy offers a timid “Sorry” as the salesgirl kneels, “Happens all the time.” Mabel bites back her temper and moves them on. “You want your dress? Then stop making chaos.”

In children’s, Cindy finds a rack of party dresses and dashes to the fitting room. Mabel waits, mind wandering as shoppers swirl with purpose around endless racks. Josh starts tugging at a clothing rail. She rolls the stroller back. There’ll be no stopping him once he’s walking. Is this my life? I thought I’d have time to myself. She coos at him: gears turning already—conspiring before they can speak. Must they be such beasts before a smidge of humanity? Love is not a shortcut. Susy always said, No one got ruined with too much love. Don’t see it; a strong hand kept Susy steady—eventually.

“Are you mumbling about Mom? Her name is Sue,” Cindy says, tugging Mabel’s dress, the try-on done. “I know; I gave it to her.” Grams, Grammy, Grammers… the rich diversity of language. “It fits?” “Yep.” “Good. Let’s go home.”

Cindy all but skips to checkout, clutching the dress—and a tiny faux-leather purse sparkling with a winged horse. “Not today,” Mabel says. “Your mother warned me you’d want the whole store. We came for a dress.” “I’ll pay with my allowance.” “Put it down.” Cindy sprints to the line anyway. Mabel, faster than she looks, catches her. “Start behaving, or we leave with nothing—and I’ll tell your mother.” Cindy shoves the purse aside. “Fine.”

They pay for the dress and head out. The sliding doors whoosh—and an alarm shrieks. Mabel’s eyes cut to Cindy. “What did you take?” “Nothing!” A guard blocks their path. “Please step back inside.” Mabel fumbles apologies, suspects the worst of her granddaughter—until the guard asks Mabel to open her purse, then empty her pockets. Out tumble keys, wrappers, a hankie—and a new lipstick. Mabel stares. In the earlier chaos, she’d tucked it away, intending to pay, then forgot. Mortified, she stammers, “I meant to buy it.” The guard gestures to the back. Cindy rushes to the cosmetics clerk. “Please help. Grams made a mistake.” The salesgirl nods to the guard: chaos earlier, an accident. The guard weighs it, warns Mabel, and lets them go. “I can afford it,” Mabel says softly. “I don’t steal.”

At the counter, she properly buys two lipsticks. Lesson learned. Order restored. She turns to Cindy. “Remember that cute purse? Go find it. It’ll go with your dress.” And off Cindy runs, the day’s battles briefly won.
